description: Exact documented palettes from landmark pixel art games. Hex values sourced from sprite rips and community documentation. Usage context included for direct application.

undertale_core.description: The universal palette used across all zones of Undertale. Toby Fox used a deliberately limited and slightly desaturated set to evoke retro SNES aesthetics while remaining readable.

colors.name: True White
colors.usage: text, UI elements, Frisk soul, snow

colors.name: Pure Red
colors.usage: HP bar, damage indicators, Undyne's spears, danger

colors.name: Pure Yellow
colors.usage: SOUL types, Mettaton EX highlights, gold

colors.name: Mid Gray
colors.usage: inactive menu items, stone textures, shadows on white

colors.name: Dark Gray
colors.usage: floor tiles, ambient fill, cave areas

colors.name: Orange
colors.usage: Hotland environment, neutral attacks (dodge or take)

colors.name: Brown
colors.usage: Ruins dirt paths, wooden elements, Snowdin tree trunks

undertale_core.notes: Undertale intentionally uses near-primary colors at full saturation for game-mechanic colors (red = harm, yellow = SOUL, cyan = blue attacks) to make the game rules readable at a glance. Environmental colors are more muted. This separation between mechanical color and aesthetic color is a key design lesson.

undertale_ruins.mood: Warm, cramped, ancient. Brown tones with amber warmth convey age and abandonment.

undertale_snowdin.mood: Cold, lonely, but with pockets of warmth. Blue-white palette with warm orange lamps creates the classic cold-night-with-light-inside feeling.

undertale_waterfall.mood: Mysterious, serene, otherworldly. The zone uses a near-monochromatic blue palette with glowing cyan accents to suggest deep underground magic.

omori_white_space.description: White Space is OMORI's psychological safe room — a featureless void with near-pure white walls and minimal objects. The palette is extremely restricted to amplify the emptiness.

colors.name: White Space White
colors.usage: walls, floor, everything — the dominant 'nothing' color

colors.name: Outline Black
colors.usage: OMORI's silhouette, meow meow, objects, text

colors.name: Near White
colors.usage: floor shadow suggestion, very subtle depth

colors.name: Light Gray
colors.usage: OMORI's room objects, laptop, light shadow

colors.name: Mid Gray
colors.usage: OMORI's shirt, subtle shadows on light objects

omori_white_space.notes: White Space's power comes from its near-total absence of color. OMORI's design in black-and-white is deliberate — he is the only defined element in the void. The red intrusions feel violently wrong specifically because the palette is otherwise colorless.

omori_headspace.description: The dream world uses a saturated, storybook palette deliberately at odds with realism. Colors are bright and slightly desaturated at the edges to create a painted-book feel.
omori_headspace.notes: Headspace uses a children's picture book palette — deliberate, childlike, and overly safe-feeling. The saturation is high but the hues are slightly shifted toward pastels to avoid looking garish. This deliberate 'too perfect' quality is part of the psychological horror — it's a constructed reality.

colors.name: Floor Grid
colors.usage: tile grid lines on white floor

colors.name: Window Blue
colors.usage: window glass, sky seen through window

colors.name: Pokecenter Red
colors.usage: Pokemon Center distinctive roof and accents

colors.name: Shadow Dark
colors.usage: deep shadow under furniture, doorway recesses

pokemon_bw_indoor.notes: Pokemon B/W indoor tiles are clean and high-contrast because the DS screen was small and bright. The floor grid (white + gray lines) creates infinite scalability — the same tile reads at any scale. Rooms feel furnished but not cluttered due to the 16x16 constraint forcing deliberate object placement.

pokemon_bw_outdoor.notes: B/W outdoor palettes use higher saturation than indoor to convey natural vibrancy. The grass tiles use a 3-tone ramp (dark/mid/light) on a neutral ground to suggest height variation without actual 3D. Routes feel spacious because paths and tall grass have high contrast.

chrono_trigger.paletteConstraint: SNES: 15 colors per background layer + transparency per palette set
chrono_trigger.description: Chrono Trigger by Akira Toriyama and Square's A-Team used the SNES color system to its absolute maximum. Each era (600 AD, 1000 AD, 2300 AD, 65000000 BC) has a distinct palette that signals time period instantly.
chrono_trigger.notes: Chrono Trigger's era-based palette system is a masterclass in environmental storytelling. Players learn to read time period from color alone within minutes. The SNES 15-color-per-layer constraint forced creative solutions — complex scenes use multiple background layers with separate palettes blended together.

earthbound.description: EarthBound uses one of the most psychedelic and rule-breaking palettes in SNES history. The game's 'Hypno flash' battle backgrounds and its use of dithering for backgrounds created its unique visual fingerprint.

colors.name: Ness Yellow
colors.usage: Ness cap stripe, golden accents, sunny tones

colors.name: Ness Red
colors.usage: Ness hat and shirt dominant color

battleBackgrounds.description: EarthBound's battle backgrounds are scrolling swirling color fields — not static images. They use palette-cycling and sine-wave movement to create hypnotic backgrounds without any additional memory cost.
colors: cycling through environment palette at high speed
battleBackgrounds.technique: Palette cycling with scroll registers — 0 actual background art used

earthbound.notes: EarthBound's palette choices are intentionally suburban-American, in sharp contrast to the medieval/fantasy settings typical of SNES RPGs. The 'normal' palette is a deliberate design choice to make the psychedelic/horror elements hit harder by contrast.

stardew_valley.description: ConcernedApe created an extraordinarily rich palette to simulate the seasonal, time-of-day, and weather variety of farm life. The palette has hundreds of colors across all assets but individual sprites stay within 8-16 colors.
stardew_valley.notes: Stardew Valley's visual variety despite small sprites comes from its seasonal palette system — the same tile art takes on completely different moods by shifting the environment palette. The game also uses transparency-based fog/rain overlays that shift perceived saturation without changing underlying sprite data.

celeste_mountain.description: Celeste uses a zone-based palette system where each chapter has its own dominant color mood. The mountain progression from Chapter 1 to Summit is also a lightness progression — darker and colder as you go up.

chapter1_forsaken_city.name: Snow White
chapter1_forsaken_city.usage: snow on platforms, Madeline hair highlight

celeste_mountain.notes: Celeste's palette philosophy is that each world state (normal Madeline vs Badeline vs Theo) should be readable from color alone. Madeline = red-purple, Badeline = deep purple with red glow, Theo = warm blue-teal. The chapter backgrounds follow an emotional temperature progression from cold-purple (depression/anxiety metaphor) to warm-gold (acceptance) at the summit.

final_fantasy_vi.paletteConstraint: SNES Mode 7 for world map, Mode 1 for standard play
final_fantasy_vi.description: FF6's character sprites are 16x24 pixels in battle (larger than overworld). The game is renowned for its use of the SNES palette to suggest complex lighting and emotional states.

environmentColors.name: Kefka Orange
environmentColors.usage: Kefka's warped god form, apocalypse sky

final_fantasy_vi.notes: FF6's palette is distinctive for using the SNES's sprite-layer transparency to create layered lighting effects, particularly in the Opera scene and the final dungeon. The World of Ruin intentionally shifts to more desaturated, darker tones to signal the apocalyptic shift — the same engine and art assets look fundamentally different by changing the palette.

aria_of_sorrow.description: Aria of Sorrow pushed GBA hardware to its visual limit. The castle environments use careful use of the GBA's limited backlight brightness and palette system to create atmosphere.
aria_of_sorrow.notes: Aria of Sorrow uses one of the GBA's most sophisticated palette systems. Because the GBA screen had poor contrast without backlight, the game compensates with extremely high contrast ratios between lit and shadowed areas. The castle reads because bright lit elements (whites, golds) are placed against very dark backgrounds — minimal midtones on architectural elements, saving midtones for character sprites.

rampConstruction.description: Each color used in a sprite needs a ramp: shadow, base, highlight. 4-step ramps add a second shadow or second highlight.
rampConstruction.hueShifting: Shadows should shift slightly toward blue/purple. Highlights should shift slightly toward yellow/orange. Pure grayscale ramps look flat.

colorCount.boss_sprite: 16-24 colors, sometimes more for large multi-part bosses
colorCount.background_layer: 8-15 colors per layer (hardware constraint on retro, convention on modern)

contrast.betweenCharacterAndBackground: Characters should be at least 3:1 contrast with the environments they appear in most
contrast.outlineVsBackground: Outlines should be the darkest color in the sprite, minimum 2:1 contrast with background
